topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with a sickening thud. My heart began to hammer against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm I couldn't control. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly above the keyboard. The headline screamed my name, a piece I had poured months of late nights and soul into. But the byline? It belonged to someone else. A stranger. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, making it hard to draw a full breath. I reread the text, searching for any anomaly, any slip-up that would explain this impossible situation. Every sentence felt tainted, a forgery. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of my apartment, running a hand through my already disheveled hair. What do I do now? The silence of the room amplified the frantic thumping in my ears.

[story 2]

When the notification popped up, Sarah froze mid-sip of her lukewarm coffee. Her hand, holding the mug, started to shake visibly. She stared at the screen, her eyes wide and unblinking. The words confirming the plagiarism hit her like a physical blow. A sudden chill swept over her, making goosebumps rise on her arms despite the warmth of the room. She slowly lowered the mug, placing it on the coaster with painstaking care. Her breathing hitched, shallow and quick. "No," she whispered, the sound barely audible in the quiet office. She leaned back in her chair, the wood groaning beneath her weight, and began to rub her temples vigorously, trying to physically smooth out the sudden turbulence churning inside her.

[story 3]

He reread the publication details three times, his gaze darting back and forth across the screen as if the answer might change if he looked hard enough. A dry, tight feeling settled in his chest, a pressure building behind his sternum. He picked up his phone, his thumb hovering over the contact list, but he couldn't bring himself to call anyone yet. Instead, he started meticulously organizing the files on his desktop, a compulsive need to impose order on the chaos unfolding in his mind. Each click of the mouse sounded deafeningly loud in the stillness. He leaned his forehead against the cool glass of the monitor, fighting the urge to simply close his eyes and retreat from the stark reality presented on the screen.
